i1.name: Angle of incidence at first surface

i2.name: Angle of emergence at second surface

locked_facts: Angle of deviation: δ = (i₁ + i₂) - A, where A is the prism angle
At minimum deviation: i₁ = i₂ (ray passes symmetrically through the prism)
Prism formula: n = sin((A + δ_min)/2) / sin(A/2)
Dispersion: n_violet > n_red, so violet bends most and red bends least (VIBGYOR order)
Thin prism approximation: δ = (n - 1)A, valid for small prism angles
Angular dispersion = δ_violet - δ_red; dispersive power ω = (n_v - n_r)/(n_y - 1)
truth_statements: The deviation produced by a prism depends on the angle of incidence, the angle of the prism, and the refractive index of the prism material
At minimum deviation, the ray passes symmetrically through the prism with the angle of incidence equal to the angle of emergence
The splitting of white light into its component colours by a prism is called dispersion, caused by the wavelength dependence of the refractive index
Violet light has the highest refractive index in glass and deviates the most, while red light has the lowest refractive index and deviates the least
The refractive index of the prism material can be accurately determined from the minimum deviation angle using n = sin((A + δ_min)/2) / sin(A/2)

aha_moment.what_to_show: White light enters the prism and emerges as a VIBGYOR spectrum. Show the refractive index values: n_violet = 1.532, n_red = 1.514. The difference is only 0.018 — yet it produces a clearly visible spread of colours! Each colour was always present in white light. The prism does not create colours; it merely separates them because glass slows violet light slightly more than red light. A second inverted prism recombines the colours back into white light — proving the colours were separated, not created.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: VIBGYOR fan emerging from the prism with refractive index labels next to each colour. Show the n difference is tiny (0.018) but the visual effect is dramatic. Optionally show Newton's experiment: second inverted prism recombines the spectrum.

simulation_states.label: White light enters the prism
simulation_states.description: Triangular prism drawn with angle A = 60°. A white light beam approaches from the left. The first refraction at the entry surface is shown with the angle of incidence i₁ marked.
simulation_states.emphasis: White light is a mixture of all visible wavelengths. The prism will separate them because the refractive index depends on wavelength.

simulation_states.label: Deviation and the i₁-δ curve
simulation_states.description: A single colour (monochromatic) ray passes through the prism. The deviation δ = (i₁ + i₂) - A is shown. As i₁ changes, δ traces a U-shaped curve with a minimum at i₁ = i₂. The minimum deviation angle δ_min is highlighted.
simulation_states.emphasis: The deviation is minimum when the ray passes symmetrically: i₁ = i₂. At this point, the prism formula gives n = sin((A + δ_min)/2) / sin(A/2). This is how we measure the refractive index of glass.

simulation_states.label: Dispersion — VIBGYOR spectrum emerges
simulation_states.description: White light enters; the full VIBGYOR spectrum exits as a fan of colours. Violet at the bottom (most deviated), red at the top (least deviated). Refractive indices shown for each colour. The angular dispersion between violet and red is marked.
simulation_states.emphasis: n_violet > n_red, so violet bends more. The tiny difference in refractive index (about 0.018 for glass) creates a visible spread of colours. Dispersive power ω = (n_v - n_r)/(n_y - 1).

simulation_states.label: Interactive — explore deviation and dispersion
simulation_states.description: Sliders to change the angle of incidence, prism angle, and refractive index. Watch the deviation change and find the minimum deviation experimentally. Toggle between monochromatic and white light to see dispersion.
simulation_states.emphasis: Find δ_min by adjusting i₁. Calculate n from the prism formula. Switch to white light to see the spectrum. Try different prism angles to see how dispersion changes.

common_misconceptions.belief: The prism creates colours — white light is pure and colourless
trigger_phrases: white light mein colour nahi hota
common_misconceptions.correction: The prism does NOT create colours. White light already contains all colours (wavelengths) mixed together. The prism merely separates them because different wavelengths travel at different speeds in glass (different n values). Newton proved this by passing the spectrum through a second inverted prism — the colours recombined into white light.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show Newton's two-prism experiment: first prism disperses white light into VIBGYOR, second inverted prism recombines the spectrum back into white light. This proves colours were always present in white light.

common_misconceptions.belief: All colours exit the prism at the same angle — they just look different
trigger_phrases: sab colours same angle se nikalte hain
common_misconceptions.correction: Different colours exit at DIFFERENT angles because each colour has a different refractive index in glass. Violet (n ≈ 1.532) deviates the most, red (n ≈ 1.514) deviates the least. The difference is small in n but produces a visible angular spread — this is dispersion.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the exit fan clearly with angle labels for violet and red. Display the n values next to each colour. The difference in n is small (~0.018) but produces a visible spread of colours.

common_misconceptions.belief: Red light bends the most because it has the most energy
trigger_phrases: red has more energy so bends more
common_misconceptions.correction: Red light actually bends the LEAST because it has the lowest refractive index in glass. Violet bends the most despite having higher energy. The bending is determined by n (which depends on wavelength), not by energy. Higher frequency (violet) means higher n in glass, which means more bending.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show VIBGYOR fan with violet at the bottom (most deviated) and red at the top (least deviated). Label the refractive indices clearly to show n_violet > n_red.

legend.meaning: Angle of prism (between the two refracting surfaces)
Minimum angle of deviation (when i₁ = i₂)
Angles of incidence and emergence at the two surfaces
Violet, Indigo, Blue, Green, Yellow, Orange, Red
